title: True Positives in Detection
concept: A predicted box is a True Positive if it has the correct class label and an IoU overlap with the ground truth box above a set threshold.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes the RPN faster than Selective Search?

options.text: It runs on the GPU using parallel matrix operations

exercises.explain: GPU acceleration allows the RPN to compute thousands of anchor proposals in milliseconds.

cards.title: Is That Box Correct?
cards.body: If you ask a model to find dogs, and it predicts a perfect bounding box around a tree and labels it 'dog', it's wrong. If it finds a dog but the box is huge and covers the whole backyard, it's also wrong. We need a strict rule to define when a box is correct.

cards.title: True Positives (TP)
cards.body: In object detection, a prediction is classified as a True Positive (TP) if: 1) the predicted class label matches the ground truth label, and 2) the IoU overlap between the predicted box and the ground truth box is greater than or equal to a set threshold (typically 0.5).

cards.title: Target Practice
cards.body: Imagine target shooting. To score a hit (True Positive), two things must happen: you must shoot the correct target (correct class), and your bullet hole must land inside the target ring (IoU above the threshold).

cards.title: Evaluating a Hit
cards.body: A model predicts a 'car' box with an IoU of 0.65 with a ground truth car. The threshold is 0.5. Since the class matches and the IoU is above 0.5, this is a True Positive. If the class was 'bus', it would be a False Positive!

cards.title: Confidence alone is not enough
cards.body: You might think a prediction with 99% confidence is automatically correct. In evaluation, if a 99% confidence box does not overlap the real object (IoU < 0.5), it is marked as a **False Positive**, penalizing the model's score.

cards.title: Correct vs. Incorrect Detections
cards.body: Visualizing true positives vs false positives in detection:

left.text:
Correct label AND high IoU.
e.g. "Dog" box with IoU = 0.72

right.text:
Incorrect label OR low IoU.
e.g. "Dog" box with IoU = 0.15

exercises.prompt: What two conditions must a predicted bounding box meet to be classified as a True Positive (TP)?
exercises.explain: A True Positive requires matching the category label and overlapping the real object's box above the threshold (e.g. IoU >= 0.5).

options.text: Correct class label and an IoU overlap above the evaluation threshold
Correct color schema and image size
High learning rate and small batch size
It must be the only box in the image

exercises.prompt: True or False: A predicted box that perfectly surrounds a cat but is labeled as 'dog' is still a True Positive.
exercises.explain: False. Even if the box coordinates are perfect, if the classification label is incorrect, it is a False Positive.
Predictions that do not align with target objects are False Positives.
exercises.sentence: A high-confidence prediction in the wrong location (IoU < 0.5) is graded as a ___ Positive.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction represents a True Positive (evaluation threshold = 0.50)?
exercises.explain: Option A has matching class labels ('car') and a high IoU (0.78 >= 0.5), making it a True Positive.

options.text: Prediction: 'Car' at coordinates, IoU with real car = 0.78
Prediction: 'Dog' at coordinates, IoU with real cat = 0.90
Prediction: 'Car' at coordinates, IoU with real car = 0.12

exercises.prompt: A model predicts a 'person' bounding box. The box has an IoU of 0.55 with a real person, and the confidence score is 92%. The evaluation threshold is set to 0.50. How is this prediction classified?

options.text: True Positive (TP), because the class matches and the IoU is above the threshold.
False Positive (FP), because the IoU is too close to the boundary.
False Negative (FN), because the confidence is not 100%.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The class matches ('person') and the IoU of 0.55 meets the threshold requirement of 0.50, qualifying it as a True Positive. Next: let's learn how we evaluate precision and recall together!
